title: Unconventional mixed state in the nematic superconductor LiFeAs
authors: G. Lamura, T. Winyard, P. Gentile, M. Speight, F. Anger, B. Buchner, S. Wurmehl, T. Shiroka
abstract: In the mixed state of type-II bulk superconductors, the magnetic field penetrates in the form of vortices enclosing one magnetic flux quantum: this is the conventional Abrikosov vortex lattice. Here, by using transverse muon-spin spectroscopy, we demonstrate the presence of an unconventional vortex lattice in LiFeAs single crystals. We also show evidence that the new mixed phase consists of stripes of "coreless" vortices, which are bound states of two spatially separated half-quantum vortices.
summary: This paper reports the discovery of an unconventional vortex lattice in the bulk superconductor LiFeAs. By analyzing muon-spin spectroscopy data, the authors find evidence of 'coreless' vortices, which are bound states of half-quantum vortices. They demonstrate that as temperature increases, these complex skyrmion chains transition into a conventional Abrikosov vortex lattice. This work provides a rare experimental glimpse into the complex topological textures possible in multi-component superconducting order parameters.
main_problem: The study seeks experimental evidence for unconventional, coreless (skyrmionic) vortex states in the bulk of the nematic superconductor LiFeAs.
main_result: Using muon-spin spectroscopy, the authors demonstrate an unconventional vortex lattice characterized by peak splitting in the magnetic field distribution, which evolves from a skyrmion chain phase to a standard Abrikostov lattice with increasing temperature.
method: The researchers combine transverse-field muon-spin spectroscopy (TF-muSR) with Ginzburg-Landau numerical simulations using gradient descent and Newton flow methods to minimize Gibbs free energy.
model_or_system: The physical system is the nematic superconductor LiFeAs, modeled using a multi-component Ginzburg-Landau theory with a complex vector order parameter and strong spin-orbit coupling.
key_observables: Local magnetic field probability distribution p(B), depolarization rate (sigma), diamagnetic shift, and the topological winding number of the skyrmion field.
important_parameters: Temperature (1.6 K to 6 K), magnetic field orientation (parallel to a-axis, 45 degrees, and c-axis), and the superconducting gap (approx 2.01-2.57 meV).
main_assumptions: The Ginzburg-Landau model is treated as an effective theory for qualitative predictions rather than precise quantitative accuracy; the simulation assumes a periodic lattice structure.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of coreless vortices; Figure 2 displays crystal structure and muSR configurations alongside peak splitting in the field distribution; Figure 4 presents contour plots of the skyrmion lattice solutions including order parameter magnitudes and local magnetic fields.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of unconventional vortices, describes the LiFeAs system and muSR experimental setup, presents the observation of peak splitting and anisotropy, details the Ginzburg-Landau numerical modeling and simulation results, and concludes with the interpretation of the temperature-dependent phase evolution.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Coreless (skyrmion) vortex, consisting of a bound state of two spatially separated HQVs with independent order parameters and phases. The cores of the two HQVs are spanned by the respec- tive supercurrents J1 and J2: HQV1 is crossed by J2 and visa-versa, thus lacking a core where both OPs are zero simultaneously. This can be seen in the bottom panel as a cross-section in the splitting direction of the two independent OPs and the resulting local field Bloc is shown. Such bound state still carries a single quantum of magnetic flux Φ0 as a whole.

FIG. 2. TF-µSR experiments. Panel A: Crystal structure of LiFeAs (cyan, gray, and red spheres represent Li, Fe, and As atoms, respectively) and relative directions of the applied magnetic field(i). Experimental configuration: the sample is glued using vacuum grease to a thin copper foil on the µSR sample-holder. This fixes its a-axis direction at low temperature, while allowing it to be changed at 300 K. The magnetic field Bapp was applied parallel to the a-axis (ii), at 45◦from it (iii), and parallel to the c-axis (iv). Panel B: TF-µSR polarization above and below Tc in a magnetic field applied parallel to the a-axis (Ba0) (a), at 45 degrees from it (B45) (b), and parallel to the c-axis...

FIG. 3. µSR relaxation — in-plane vs. out-of-plane results. Temperature evolution of the fit parameters, as resulting from fits of the experimental asymmetry in the time domain [25]: (a) TF-µSR depolarization rate in a magnetic field applied in the ab-plane (Ba0, Ba45) and out-of-plane (Bc). (b) Diamagnetic field shift recorded in all the applied-field configurations.

FIG. 4. Contour plots of the SC skyrmion chains (i.e., lattice solutions) for the model given by Eq. (2). From left to right: OP magnitudes |ψ1| and |ψ2|, relative phase θ12, local magnetic field strength Bloc, and its probability density p(B).
